Optimal Codeword Cons
Finite A
†
Anupam Chattopadhyay
†
School of Computer Science and Engineering,
‡
Uniklinik Aachen, RWTH A
Abstract—Biomolecular computation has emerged as an im-
portant  area of  computer  science  research due  to  its high
2 information density, immense parallelism opportunity along with
2 potential applications in cryptography, genetic engineering and
0 bioinformatics. Computational frameworks using DNA molecules
2 have been proposed in the literature to accomplish varied tasks
 
such as simulating logical operations, performing matrix multi-
n
plication, and encoding instances of NP-hard problems. In one of
u
the key applications, several studies have proposed construction
J
  of ﬁnite automata using DNA hybridisation and ligation [1][2].
4 The state and symbol encoding of these ﬁnite automata are done
 
  manually. In this manuscript, we study the codeword construction
]
problem for this approach. We derive exact theoretical bounds
L on the number of symbols and states in the ﬁnite automata and
F also obtain the complete set of symbols in a speciﬁc case, thereby
.
s solving an open problem posed in [1]. For automatic encoding,
c two different solutions, based on a heuristic and on Integer Linear
[ Programming (ILP),  are proposed. Furthermore, we propose
 
  an early simulation-based validation of laboratory experiments.
1 Our proposed ﬂow accepts a ﬁnite automaton,  automatically
v encodes the symbols for the actual experiments and executes
6 the simulation step-by-step.
2
9
I.  INTRODUCTION
1
0  The ﬁeld of DNA computing was initiated by the landmark
.
6 work of Adleman [3] who showed how to solve small instances
0 of the NP-complete Hamiltonian Path problem by encoding
2
them on DNA strands. DNA,  or Deoxyribonucleic Acid,
2
: is a fundamental unit  that encodes the genetic information
v that makes life possible.  It  is a polymer whose monomeric
i
units consist of four nucleotides: Adenine (A), Cytosine (C),
X
r Guanine (G) and Thymine (T). The letters of  this alphabet
a bind according to the well-known Watson-Crick complement
condition: Adenine binds to Thymine (A ↔ T) and Cytosine
binds to Guanine (C ↔ G).
GGATG(N)9… 
CCTAC(N)13… 
GGATGNNNNNNNNN ATGGTATTA… 
CCTACNNNNNNNNNTACCATAAT… 
FokI restriction enzyme 
sticky end 
GGATGNNNNNNNNN           ATGGTATTA… 
CCTACNNNNNNNNNTACC           ATAAT… 
Fig. 1.  Restriction Enzyme Operation
Typically, to use DNA molecules to perform computations,

truction for DNA-based
utomata
‡
and Arnab Chakrabarti
Nanyang Technological University, Singapore
achen University, Germany
problem instances have to be encoded onto a set of DNA
molecules. These molecules are then allowed to react with or
without  the inﬂuence of suitable enzymes to form potential
solutions. Finally one uses protocols such as gel electrophore-
sis  to obtain solutions  in molecular  form. Some of  these
biomolecular reactions include:
Hybridisation: A double-stranded DNA molecule is formed
by creating hydrogen bonds between the complementary bases
of two single-stranded molecules that have opposite orienta-
tions.
Polymerase Enzyme: Polymerase are DNA enzymes which
help duplicate a DNA strand. A Polymerase Chain Reaction
(PCR) is an oft-used protocol for detecting or amplifying a
speciﬁc molecule within a large mix of molecules.
Ligation: If a double stranded DNA molecule contains a
broken bond in one of the strands then it can be glued by a
ligase enzyme such as T4. The process is known as ligation.
Restriction Endonuclease/Enzyme: A restriction enzyme
recognizes a particular sequence of nucleotides in a double
stranded DNA and cuts  it  into  two pieces by destroying
the phosphodiester bonds at  a speciﬁc distance  from the
recognition site. This is essentially the inverse operation of
ligation. The single strands hanging out of each piece are
known as sticky ends. The recognition site as well as  the
distance from it to the cutting point are inherent properties of
a restriction enzyme [4], [5]. An example of such as a reaction
via the restriction enzyme FokI is shown in Figure 1. FokI
cuts the base strand from its recognition site at a distance of
nine nucleotides and the complementary strand at a distance
of thirteen nucleotides producing a sticky end consisting of
four nucleotides. Several restriction enzymes can also perform
blunt cuts which do not produce any sticky end.
Gel Electrophoresis: This technique allows separating the
DNA molecules according to their weight (or length). This is
used for controlling the intermediate steps of a reaction or for
reading the ﬁnal output.
In the original paper by Adleman [3], a number of  the
aforementioned steps were employed. The high information
density and massive parallelism of DNA computing sparked
major research interest in this ﬁeld, though it was shown that
DNA computing does not offer any additional power from the
perspective of computability theory.

Several NP-hard problems
were subsequently encoded into DNA molecules [6] including
the well-known Circuit Satisﬁability problem [7],  [8]. Sev-

eral  important practical applications followed including the
breaking of the Data Encryption Standard (DES) [9] and the
possibility of parallel algorithms for integer factorization [10],
a key step in several public key cryptography protocols. This
initiated the ﬁeld of DNA-based cryptography [11]. A detailed
survey of DNA computing is presented in [12]. A serious
disadvantage of  the encoding approaches proposed in [3],
[7]  is that  the number of DNA strands required to encode
instances grows exponentially with their size (see Table 1
in [7]). There have been some advances in encoding problem
instances of modest  sizes. For  instance, Braich et al.  [13]
show how to solve a twenty-variable 3-SAT instance. However
encoding large problem instances so that all potential solutions
are simultaneously constructed, as is the approach in [13], is
difﬁcult since, molecular computations are inherently error-
prone and this affects the reliability of the ﬁnal result [14].
Recent research in DNA computing has, therefore, focused on
the construction of small nanodevices that can be scaled up
for solving practical problems [15], [16].
A. Finite Automata using DNA Computing
The computing power of biomolecular nanodevices that are
capable of self-assembly was studied by Winfree et al. [17].
They showed that there can be different self-assembly prim-
itives  for DNA molecules  that  can express  regular gram-
mars (ﬁnite automata), context-free grammars and unrestricted
grammars.  In particular,  it was shown that a 2-dimensional
self-assembly of DNA molecules can simulate a Universal
Turing Machine. This study spear-headed experimental work
to validate these models.
Benenson et al. [2] were the ﬁrst to propose constructing a
ﬁnite state automaton with DNA molecules by using restriction
enzymes. Their model was simple enough to be used in a
genetic control study [18] with far-reaching implications in
genetic engineering, pharmaceutical experiments and medical
diagnosis.  Subsequently,  experiments with  transducers  i.e.
ﬁnite automata with outputs, have been reported in [19] and
there has been some success in 2-dimensional self-assembly
of DNA tiles [20]. Based on experimentally validated ﬁnite
automata constructions, novel  theoretical models  for push-
down automata have also been proposed [21], [22], [23].
The simplicity of  the ﬁnite automata construction using
restriction enzymes motivated researchers  to scale up  the
problem size. Experimental studies with an autonomous, pro-
grammable 3-symbol,  3-state ﬁnite automata was  reported
in [1]. The authors of this paper claimed that their approach
can be used to construct a 37-symbol, 3-state ﬁnite automata
(a bound that we improve in this paper). This design approach
led to interesting applications in molecular image encryption
by using a 2-state, 2-symbol state machine [24].
B. Codeword Design for DNA Computing
Codeword construction for DNA is an well-studied topic
due to its importance in tasks like DNA-based information
storage, DNA computing or molecular bar code reading for
chemical libraries. A good codeword construction aim towards

increasing information density and/or parallelism in computing
without  creating cross-hybridisation between distinct  code-
words. The goal of maximizing the information density can be
reduced to the problem of ﬁnding large sets of DNA strands
maximally separated in a metric space, where strands with
high hybridization afﬁnity are mapped to neighboring points.
In this setting,  it  is proved that codeword design is an NP-
complete problem [25]. Consequently, various heuristics have
been proposed for DNA codeword design,  including generic
algorithm [26], taboo search [27], stochastic search [28], and
also drew inspiration from the traditional problem of binary
codeword construction [29]. A survey of DNA codeword
construction challenges and approaches is presented in [30].
Often  DNA  codeword  design  entails  satisfying
much  stronger  constraints  than  enforced  by  pure  non-
hybridization  [31][32].  Such  combinatorial  constraints
often originate  from the use case,  e.g., DNA-based self-
assembly or ﬁnite automata construction,  as we  focus  in
this manuscript. Depending on the experimental  approach,
different  combinatorial  constraints and different  codeword
design strategy are needed, as described in, e.g., [33][34].
In this work, we focus on the codeword construction for
ﬁnite automata using a single restriction enzyme, as proposed
in [1]. To the best of our knowledge,  this has not been at-
tempted before, thus leaving several interesting research prob-
lems unresolved. The problem of codeword construction for a
similar setting, with multiple restriction enzymes, is discussed
in [33].

For recombinase-based state machines (RSMs),  the
codeword construction problem is recently proposed in [35].
It  is also imperative to prepare a simulation environment
before undertaking the actual experiment. A generic simula-
tion ﬂow for DNA computing was developed in [36] where
one can model and simulate various bio-molecular reactions.
However, the problem of constructing distinguished languages
(the encoding problem) was not addressed there. A design
automation environment for Boolean circuit construction via
DNA computing was developed in [37].
In this perspective, our main contributions in this paper are
outlined below.
• For arbitrary p and l, we  identify theoretical bounds
on the number of valid words and the size of a (p, l)-
distinguished language. This allows us to determine the
maximum number of symbols and (state, symbol) pairs
possible for restriction enzymes-based constructions of
ﬁnite automata.
• We design efﬁcient algorithms that enumerate an optimal
or a near-optimal sized (p, l)-distinguished language.
• We also develop a simulation ﬂow to allow dry experi-
ments and validation.
The rest of  the paper  is organized as follows. Section II
introduces  the formal  structure of  the problem. Section III
contains a detailed example of a ﬁnite automaton construction
using DNA molecules. The encoding problem is dealt with
in Section IV and we report experimental results about  the
efﬁciency of our automation and the construction of ﬁnite
automata with a larger state count  than reported in [1]  in.

Section V. Section VI contains some concluding remarks.
Supplementary material with the actual symbol enumeration
is presented in Appendix.
II. PRELIMINARIES AND MOTIVATION
In this section, we brieﬂy state some of the basic deﬁnitions
and terminology that are used in this paper.
A ﬁnite state machine (FSM) is a quintuple (Σ, S, s0, δ, F),
where
• Σ is the input alphabet (a ﬁnite, non-empty set of sym-
bols);
• S is a ﬁnite, non-empty set of states;
• s0 ∈ S is the initial state;
• δ : S ×Σ → S is the state-transition function;
• F ⊆ S is the set of ﬁnal states (possibly empty).
To simulate an FSM using DNA molecules, we encode its
symbols using the alphabet Σ = {A, C, G, T} which com-
prises of the letters representing the nucleotides that constitute
DNA. Note that there are two alphabets under consideration
here: the alphabet of the FSM and the alphabet that we use to
encode the states and symbols of the FSM. To avoid confusion,
we use the term symbol  to denote an element of  the input
alphabet of an FSM and letter to denote an element of Σ, the
encoding alphabet.
In what follows, ﬁx Σ = {A, C, G, T}. Given a positive
p
integer p, we denote by Σ  the set of all words of length p
from the alphabet Σ. The set of all words from Σ is denoted
∗  p
by Σ . For w = a1a2 · · · ap ∈ Σ  and i ≤ j, deﬁne w[i, j]
to be the subword of w that starts at  index i and ends at
index j,  that  is, w[i, j] = aiai+1 . . . aj . Given an integer
1 ≤ l ≤ p, we let subl(w) denote the set of p−l+1 subwords
w[1, l], w[2, l+1], . . . , w[p−l+1, p] of length l. Given a word
∗
w = a1 · · · ar ∈ Σ , we deﬁne its complement to be the word
w¯ = a¯1 · · · a¯r, where a¯i  is the Watson-Crick complement of
the letter ai,  that  is, A and T are complements and so are
C and G. To capture this notion in general, we introduce
the notion of a paired alphabet. A pairing P ⊆ Σ × Σ of
an alphabet Σ is deﬁned as a set of tuples such that every
a ∈ Σ appears  in exactly one  tuple once. We  then call
(Σ, P) a paired alphabet—in our settings this will be given
by ({A, C, T, G}, {(A, T), (C, G), (T, A), (G, C)}). For sake
of  readability and a slight abuse of notation, we will use
Σ to denote a paired alphabet  in the following. Under  this
deﬁnition, the notion of complements is easily extensible by
choosing the letter-wise complement as the pairs appearing in
the pairing and using the above extension to words. We then
denote by compl(w) the set of complements of the words in
p
the set subl(w). A word w = a1, . . . , ap ∈ Σ is a palindrome
if for all 1 ≤ i ≤ (cid:98)p/2(cid:99), ai is the Watson-Crick complement
of ap−i+1.
Note that this deﬁnition of a palindrome is different from the
usual deﬁnition for words where the letters ai and ap−i+1 are
required to be identical. In DNA computing, one is interested
in preventing nucleotide strands  to fold and form hairpin
loop (alternatively known as stem-loop) structures [38]. In the

domain of microbiology research such palindromic sequences
are also referred to as Inverted Repeat (IR) [39].
p
Let us ﬁx integers p and l with 1 ≤ l ≤ p. A word w ∈ Σ
is (p, l)-valid if it satisﬁes the following conditions:
1)  |subl(w) ∪ compl(w)| = 2 · (p − l + 1);
2) no word in subl(w) is a palindrome.
The ﬁrst condition says that the length-l subwords of w are
distinct and no two of them are complements of one another.
Thus a word is valid if  its subwords are distinct,  are not
palindromes and no two of  them are complements of one
another. Formally,  this can be captured with the notion of
p
(p, l)-distinguished language. We call a language L ⊆ Σ ,
(p, l)-distinguished if
1) every word of L is (p, l)-valid, and,
(cid:48)  (cid:48)
2)  for all w, w ∈ L with w (cid:54)= w we have that
(cid:48)  (cid:48)
(subl(w) ∪ compl(w)) ∩ (subl(w ) ∪ compl(w )) = ∅.
In this deﬁnition,  the second condition states that distinct
words  in a distinguished  language neither have  the same
subwords nor their complements. Intuitively speaking, a valid
word in our setting is used to encode a single input symbol
of the ﬁnite automaton under construction. Subwords are used
to encode the (state, symbol) pairs of the automaton. As will
be clear  later on,  the restrictions on words and languages
that were described above allow us to encode inputs to the
automaton in such a way that one can use restriction enzymes
to simulate its computation on the given input. While this
approach was outlined by Soreni et al.  [1],  they manually
1
determined words  that  encode  input  symbols.  This  leads
to the open problem that, what  is the maximum achievable
number of valid words for a (p, l)-distinguished language and
how to determine those words. For a better understanding of
the problem scope, we now look closely into an exemplary
FSM.
III. AN EXAMPLE FSM
Consider  the ﬁnite automaton shown in Figure 2 which
accepts binary strings ending with 0.

In this section we revisit
the simulation such an automaton using DNA molecules [2].
As was explained in Section II, we use the alphabet Σ =
{A, C, G, T} to encode input symbols of an automaton.
In this example we set p = 5 and l = 4 and encode
the input symbols 0, 1 by valid (5, 4)-words w0 = ATTGC
and w1 = AGTCA,  respectively. We also encode a termi-
nating symbol  t by the valid (5, 4)-word TCGTG making
sure that {w0, w1, t} is a (5, 4)-distinguished language. For
i ∈ {0, 1},  the subword wi[1, 4] represents the fact  that  the
automaton is  in state S0 and reading symbol  i;  and,  the
subword wi[2, 5] denotes that  the automaton is in state S1
and reading symbol i. In this way, all possible (state, symbol)
pairs are encoded. Since {w0, w1, t} is (5, 4)-distinguished,
the subwords w0[1, 4], w0[2, 5], w1[1, 4], w1[2, 5], t[1, 4], t[2, 5]
are all distinct, are not palindromes and no two of these are
complements of one another.
1
Further, the symbol table reported in [1], Table 1 contains overlapping 4-bp
sequences between No. 11 and No. 18. Thus all codewords are not valid..

is presented in Appendix.

III. AN EXAMPLE FSM
Consider  the ﬁnite automaton shown in Figure 2 which
accepts binary strings ending with 0.

In this section we revisit
the simulation such an automaton using DNA molecules [2].
As was explained in Section II, we use the alphabet Σ =
{A, C, G, T} to encode input symbols of an automaton.
In this example we set p = 5 and l = 4 and encode
the input symbols 0, 1 by valid (5, 4)-words w0 = ATTGC
and w1 = AGTCA,  respectively. We also encode a termi-
nating symbol  t by the valid (5, 4)-word TCGTG making
sure that {w0, w1, t} is a (5, 4)-distinguished language. For
i ∈ {0, 1},  the subword wi[1, 4] represents the fact  that  the
automaton is  in state S0 and reading symbol  i;  and,  the
subword wi[2, 5] denotes that  the automaton is in state S1
and reading symbol i. In this way, all possible (state, symbol)
pairs are encoded. Since {w0, w1, t} is (5, 4)-distinguished,
the subwords w0[1, 4], w0[2, 5], w1[1, 4], w1[2, 5], t[1, 4], t[2, 5]
are all distinct, are not palindromes and no two of these are
complements of one another.

Fig. 2.  FSM via
The input to the automaton is encoded on the base strand of
a double-stranded DNA molecule (shown on top in Figure 2).
In  this  instance,  the  string 110t  is  encoded  in  the base
strand along with additional  information to guide the action
of  the restriction enzyme which in this case is BbvI. The
second strand, called the complementary strand, consists of
complementary nucleotides that align with their partners in
the base strand. Together  these  two strands constitute our
input  tape. The restriction enzyme BbvI has  the property
that it recognizes a sequence of nucleotides (the recognition
site) which in this case is the sequence GCAGC. It cuts the
base strand eight nucleotides after the recognition site and the
complementary strand twelve nucleotides after the recognition
site. When this happens  for  the ﬁrst  time,  it  exposes  the
sequence AGTC on the base strand which as discussed above
means that the automaton is in state S0 reading the symbol 1.
This exposed sequence is called the sticky end because it
is  in  the nature of nucleotides  to form bonds with  their
complementary partners. At this point, the T4 ligase attaches
itself to this exposed end via the complementary sticky end
TCAG as  shown  in  the ﬁgure. This operation,  in effect,
deletes  the ﬁrst  input  symbol  from the input  tape because
when the restriction enzyme works on it again,  it cuts the
base strand beyond the ﬁrst  input symbol  ignoring it  in the
process. This sequence of cutting the strands by the restriction
enzyme followed by ligation continues until the whole of the
input is “consumed.” When the terminal symbols are exposed,
special nucleotide sequences called detection symbols attach
themselves to them. Depending on which sequence of terminal
symbols were exposed by the restriction enzyme, one can

DNA Operations
detect whether the automaton terminated in state S0 or S1.
In an actual experiment, one determines the termination state
by additional biochemical reactions such as polymerase chain
reactions and gel electrophoresis. In the example shown, the
automaton terminates in state S1 as it should.
IV. AUTOMATED STATE ENCODING
Having explained how one might construct an FSM using
DNA strands and restriction enzymes, we ﬁnd it convenient
to explain some the requirement on the words and languages
∗
from Σ  that we  imposed  in Section  II  in order  to be
able to encode inputs to the FSM.  In the context of (p, l)-
distinguished languages, the subwords subl(w) are the sticky
ends produced by the restriction enzymes and these represent
the (state, symbol) pairs of the automaton.
rule 1:The sticky ends, produced by the restriction enzymes,
cannot be palindromes as they would stick to one
another to form hairpin loop [38], [40].
rule 2:Each  sticky  end  represents  a  particular
(symbol, state) pair. To uniquely identify a symbol,
a sticky end cannot occur more than once within
the symbol itself or within any other symbols.
rule 3:The complement of  the sticky end,  selected for a
state of a symbol, cannot occur in the same or any
other symbol.
rule 4:All the DNA symbols should have a sequence which
is unique. So there cannot be a repetition of similar
DNA symbols in the library of valid symbols.
Note that rule 4 is encompassing rule 2. The mapping of
valid symbols to the states are followed by ﬁxed choices for the

symbols of state transitions. On the basis of these constraints,
it is possible to identify the theoretical bounds on the symbol
and state count, which is done in the following sub-section. A
speciﬁc case of this bound calculation is presented in [1].
A.

An Upper bound for FSM encoding
Assume that Lp,l is a (p, l)-distinguished language of max-
imal cardinality. A simple upper bound for |Lp,l| can be ob-
tained as follows: the total number of non-palindrome l-letter
l  (cid:98)l/2(cid:99)
subwords is given by |Σ| − |Σ|  . Excluding complement
1  l  (cid:98)l/2(cid:99)
subwords, at most 2 (|Σ| − |Σ|  ) such l-letter words can
appear as subwords in Lp,l.
As every p-letter word in a (p, l)-distinguished language
contains exactly p − l + 1 distinct subwords of length l, we
obtain the bound
l  (cid:98)l/2(cid:99)
|Σ| − |Σ|
|Lp,l| ≤  .
2(p − l + 1)
Surprisingly, this very simple limit is tight for the range of
parameters that we explored so far (cf. Table I) and we suspect
that it is tight for all p, l, except for the case of l being 1.
For a given FSM (Γ, S, s0, δ, F) and parameter p, l we then
obtain the following bounds:
|Γ| ≤ |Lp,l| − 1  (1)
|S| ≤ p − l + 1  (2)
which in turn bounds the number of transitions from above by
(|Lp,l|−1)(l −p+1). Note that we cannot use every word of
Lp,l to encode symbol-state pairs, as the construction reserves
one terminal-symbol to read out the result of the computation.
B. Problem Formulation
Let us ﬁrst formalize the optimization problem of ﬁnding a
maximal (p, l)-distinguished language over a paired alphabet
Σ.
MAX Σ-DISTINGUISHED LANGUAGE
Input:  Integers p, l
Problem:  Compute  a  (p, l)-distinguished  lan-
guage of maximal cardinality
We suspect that this problem can be solved in time poly-
p
nomial in |Σ|  , but this remains an interesting open question.
For the scope of this application we provide a heuristic that
solves this problem with reasonable solution quality.
The aforementioned constraints for valid symbols can be
conveniently represented in a graphical view, as shown in the
Figure 3. In the graph, the 4-letter symbols made out of the
alphabets {A, T, G, C} are nodes (V ). A node, V1 is connected
via a directed edge (E) with V2 only if the last 3 letters of
V1 are exactly same as the ﬁrst 3 letters of V2. The graph
constructed satisﬁes following properties.
• The graph contains several cycles. Exact enumeration of
cycle count  is also possible but, not  required for  this
work.
• The maximum indegree of any vertex (Vi) is ≤ 4.
• The maximum outdegree of any vertex (Vi) is ≤ 4.

Fig. 3.  FSM Encoding Problem
From Figure 3,  the enumeration of all valid symbols can
be alternatively viewed as enumeration of all directed, vertex-
disjoint node covers of  length 3, also known as P3-cover.
In the general case,  the number of nodes to be covered is
dependent on the value of Smax identiﬁed in subsection IV-A.
For the number of states Smax being equal to n, the problem
is to determine maximum, vertex-disjoint Pn-covers.
C. Heuristic Approach
For  the heuristic computation, we transform an instance
(Σ, p, l) into a directed auxiliary graph GA as follows:  the
vertices of GA are exactly the non-palindrome l-letter words
l  (cid:48)
of Σ and two words w, w ∈ V (GA) are connected by an arc
(cid:48)  (cid:48)
(w, w ) if w[2, l] = w [1, l −1]. Thus, a directed path in G of
p
length p−l+1 corresponds to a word in Σ without repeated or
palindrome subwords—note that such a word might be invalid,
as it could contain both a subword and its complement.
The heuristic now greedily chooses directed (p−l+1)-paths
such that all vertices on that path have a low out-degree. Every
vertex can be used at most once in this process,  thus no p-
letter subword will appear twice. However, as a word and its
complement could be chosen, we need a post-processing step
to create a valid (p, l)-distinguished language.
At ﬁrst,  the algorithm proceeds by creating an exhaustive
non-palindromic list of symbols with length l. The graph is
then constructed by creating directed edges when there is
an overlap of literals between 2 nodes. The ﬁrst while-loop
combines neighbouring symbols as long as possible. This is
controlled by an increasing outdegree. At  the beginning, all
nodes are checked and a symbol  is formed by connecting
a node with minimal outdegree  to its neighbouring node,
which also has minimal outdegree and so on. The nodes are
connected till the valid symbol size (p) is reached, which is
alternatively indicated by the maximum state count (Smax = p
- l + 1).

In case, the formation of a valid symbol is not possible
starting from the indicated node, the outdegree is increased by

ALGORITHM 1: Heuristic via Directed Path-cover
Input: p, l
Output: L
L = ∅;
GA = createGraph(p, l);
degree = 0;
while GA contains vertices with outdegree ≥ degree do
v = Vertex of minimal outdegree ≥ degree;
P = (v);
for i = 1 → (p − l) do
w = Out-neighbour of v with minimal outdegree;
P = P +w;
v = w;
end
if length(P) = p then
L = L ∪ word(GA, P);
GA = GA \ P ;
else
degree = degree + 1;
end
end
Gc = createConflictGraph(L);
while Gc contains node with degree ≥ 1 do
w = Vertex of maximum degree in Gc;
Gc = Gc −w;
L = L \ {w};
end
one and the iterative search for a valid symbol begins again. If
no such node is found, the outdegree requirement is increased
by 1 and the While-loop is iterated. This guarantees that the
algorithm is terminated when no more symbol construction is
possible. After every valid symbol generation, the correspond-
ing nodes are removed from the graph.
The last part of the algorithm enforces the rule 3 mentioned
in the section IV. During symbol construction, complementary
sticky ends may become part of the same or different symbols.
This is identiﬁed by an exhaustive pairwise check of all the
symbols and then a conﬂict graph is constructed. In the conﬂict
graph, a conﬂict edge denotes that either of  the connected
nodes (i.e. symbols) can exist in the valid symbol library. To
obtain maximum number of symbols, repeatedly nodes with
maximum degree are removed till no conﬂict edge remains.
D. Algorithmic Complexity
The worst-case execution time for  the above heuristic is
based on the assumption that all combinations of Smax nodes
are tried for constructing a valid symbol. In practice,  this is
less since,  the choice of the ﬁrst node bounds the algorithm
to search for a following node within at most 4 options.
This makes  the  total number of possible combinations as
Smax−1
n·4  , where n is the number of nodes in the graph GA.
l  (cid:100)l/2(cid:101)  l  Smax−1
For n being (4 −4  ), the complexity is O(4 ·4  ). A
l  l
similar complexity ﬁgure of O(4 · 4 ) is obtained for the sec-

ond part of the algorithm, where conﬂict edges are removed.
Despite the exponential complexity, the heuristic turned out to
be highly efﬁcient for the currently used restriction enzymes
where, l ≤ 4 as well as several other scenarios presented in
the section V.
E.  ILP Based Approach
Whereas the above heuristic yields a large set of codewords
in a reasonable amount of time, a provable maximal set of
codewords could be desirable. To this end we reduce  the
problem of ﬁnding a maximal set of codewords with word
length p and subword-length l  to ﬁnding a independent set
in a suitable conﬂict graph. As this problem is NP-hard in
general,  this approach can only work for a limited range of
parameters. However, using this formulation we were able to
calculate— among other parameters—an optimal number of
codewords for p = 6 and l = 4.
Let us describe the construction of the conﬂict graph Gp,l
in detail: the vertices of Gp,l are exactly the (p, l)-valid words
p  (cid:48)
of Σ and we add an edge between w, w ∈ V (Gp,l) if
(cid:48)  (cid:48)
(subl(w) ∪ compl(w)) ∩ (subl(w ) ∪ compl(w )) (cid:54)= ∅
(cid:48)
holds. That is: two words w, w are connected by an edge in
the conﬂict graph if not both can be in a (p, l)-distinguished
p
language L ⊆ Σ at the same time. Conversely, any set X ⊆
V (Gp,l) that is independent,  i.e. Gp,l[X] contains no edges,
is by deﬁnition (p, l)-distinguished. Thus a maximal (p, l)-
distinguished language can be found by ﬁnding a maximum
independent set in Gp,l.
As MAXIMUM INDEPENDENT SET is a well-established
problem, we leave away the ILP-formulation that can easily be
derived from a given conﬂict graph Gp,l. A maximum set of
(6, 4)-distinguished words of cardinality 40 that was obtained
solving MAXIMUM INDEPENDENT SET on the conﬂict graph
G6,4 can be found in the Appendix.
F. Transition Encoding
We explain the transition encoding by taking an exemplary
transition  from statem to staten with  the  input  symbol
being sym. The double-stranded transition molecule has the
format of {recognition site (cid:107) buffer strand (cid:107) complemen-
tary single strand}. The complementary single strand is de-
termined from the encoding of  the statem, which remains
in the sticky end after every cut by restriction enzyme. The
length of the buffer strand is allocated in such a way that the
next cut by restriction enzyme consumes the current symbol
sym and reaches the corresponding staten of the next symbol.
For every symbol,  the state encodings are done in a regular
fashion. Therefore, the staten of next symbol always requires
consumption of a ﬁxed amount of  literals. Note,  that  the
encoding for recognition site and the buffer strand must be
different from the encoding of the symbols.
G.

Software Work ﬂow
The algorithms presented in the previous section is packaged
in a software tool ﬂow. The software follows the steps outlined
in Figure 4. It accepts as input a set of restriction enzymes [4].

and an FSM. First, it checks if it is feasible to construct the
DNA-automaton. If so, an appropriate encoding of the FSM
is automatically generated using the symbols taken from the
restriction enzymes provided. Finally, the ﬁnite automaton is
simulated step by step for a given input tape.
V. EXPERIMENTAL EVALUATION
The complete tool ﬂow reported in this paper is developed
using C++, consisting of about 2600 lines of code. To solve the
ILPs the CPLEX solver was used [41]. The code was tested
on a AMD Phenom II X6 1100T 3.3 GHz 6-core processor
with 8GB RAM running 64-bit scientiﬁc Linux OS. We will
present two sets of experiments: In the ﬁrst one, a simulation
of a 5-state FSM using our tool is shown as an example and the
second is a benchmark measuring the efﬁciency of generating
optimal or near-optimal symbols for state encoding.
A. Finite Automata for Checking Divisibility by 5
With  the capabilities provided by our  tool,  that  allows
efﬁciently, automated encoding, it becomes possible to execute
FSMs with a higher number of  states and symbols  than
reported earlier. For example, one can construct a 3-state, 39-
symbol FSM using the restriction enzyme BbvI (we actually
have 40 symbols available, but we need to use one to signal-
ize termination). For a realistic non-trivial example an FSM
construction for divisibility check is chosen. The automata
accepts binary strings of 0 and 1 that  represent numbers
that are multiples of ﬁve. The automata, that can be seen in
Figure 5, has a total of 5 states and 2 symbols. This means we
have to use a symbol length of 8 for the restriction enzymes
with a sticky end of  length 4. For creating the  transition
encoding, which can consume the complete symbol, one needs
a restriction enzyme having a distance from recognition site
to the cutting point  to be ≥ 9.  If this is not  the case,  then
we might not be able to cut enough to transition between
different states. This is automatically determined by the tool
and a matching restriction enzyme FokI  is selected. The
state encoding and the transition encoding are automatically
determined within 1 second. The detailed simulation steps for
different binary strings can be found in Appendix. It should

also be noted that FSM is a powerful construction that can be
applied to many different problems, two more cases of which
are also presented in the Appendix.

B. Benchmarking Symbol Generation Efﬁciency
To determine  the efﬁciency of our proposed algorithms
for generating the state symbols, different values of p and
l  are  fed  to  the algorithm. The corresponding  theoretical
maximum are computed. The generated ILP and heuristic
based symbol counts for different p and l values are presented
in Table I. The ILP-based method could not scale for certain
values due to the increasing number of ILP constraints. But
as can be seen from the theoretical upper bound this is not
important,  since the number of possible symbols decreases
with growing p for a ﬁxed l. This may seem counter-intuitive
at ﬁrst. The reason is  that, with such a small alphabet,  it
becomes increasingly difﬁcult  to not repeat a subword with
increasing word length. Furthermore, the right enzymes must
exist to be able to use certain (p, l) pairs. To the best of our
knowledge, none that would work with larger DNA strings
than the ones for which we present  results are known. For
the parameter values with acceptable ILP constraints, the pre-
computed maximum number of symbols is always returned.
In contrast,  the heuristic approach scales well and returns
symbols, which are quite close to the optimal. The heuristic
approach took less than 1 second to generate the symbols in
all the presented cases. For ILP, the timeout was set to 1 hour,
within which several scenarios failed to return the symbols.

VI. CONCLUSION AND FUTURE WORK
In this paper, an automated and efﬁcient ﬁnite state machine
encoding approach is presented. The optimal solutions for the
number of achievable symbol and state were generated to
be used in the our tool. To ﬁnd such solutions, an efﬁcient
heuristic and an ILP-based approach are proposed, which are
veriﬁed through simulation.

formulation.
